summary: A French film movement (Nouvelle Vague) of the late 1950s and 1960s emphasizing innovative techniques (handheld cameras, natural lighting, jump cuts, non-professional actors) and auteur-driven filmmaking. Associated directors include Jean-Luc Godard, Francois Truffaut, Agnes Varda, Chris Marker, and Alain Resnais. Key films: Breathless (Godard) and The 400 Blows (Truffaut). Centered on Cahiers du Cinema criticism. Divided into Right Bank (Godard, Truffaut) and Left Bank (Marker, Resnais, Varda) factions.

works.name: Breathless / A bout de souffle (1960)
works.description: Jean-Luc Godard's seminal New Wave film. A Humphrey Bogart-obsessed protagonist named Michel kills a police officer after being caught with a stolen car, in a now-famous jarring five-shot sequence. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A Humphrey Bogart-obsessed character (Michel) kills a police officer after being caught with a stolen car, in a famous five-shot sequence
examples: In a film from this movement, a Humphrey Bogart-obsessed character kills a cop after being caught with a stolen car.
In one of the seminal films of the French New Wave, Breathless, Michel commits this crime during a jarring, now famous, five shot sequence.

works.name: Style / Characteristics
works.description: The French New Wave emphasized handheld cameras, natural lighting, jump cuts, and non-professional actors. Jean Rouch's Moi, un Noir was cited as inspiring the movement through its use of jump cuts and non-professional actors. Jean Vigo was a key predecessor and his name was given to the Prix Jean Vigo. The movement is divided into Right Bank directors (Godard, Truffaut — more popular and commercially successful) and Left Bank directors (Marker, Resnais, Varda — more experimental). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Techniques: handheld cameras, natural lighting, jump cuts, non-professional actors; self-conscious writing
examples: This two word phrase names a French film movement developed in the early 1960s that emphasized innovative cinematic techniques and self-conscious writing.
Handheld cameras, natural lighting and rebellious protagonists characterize many of the films in this country's New Wave movement

clues.clue: Left Bank directors include Chris Marker, Alain Resnais, and Agnes Varda; Right Bank includes Godard and Truffaut

works.name: Jean-Luc Godard
works.description: Right Bank French New Wave director. Made Breathless (A bout de souffle, 1960), as well as Pierrot le fou and Vivre sa vie — both starring his then-wife Anna Karina. Alphaville is described as 'neo-noir.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Directed Breathless; also directed Pierrot le fou and Vivre sa vie (both starring then-wife Anna Karina); neo-noir Alphaville
examples: the Right Bank included more popular and successful filmmakers such as this director of Pierrot le fou and Vivre sa vie, both of which star his then-wife Anna Karina
New Wave films like Le Samourai and Alphaville are often called 'neo-' this word [noir]

works.name: Francois Truffaut
works.description: Right Bank French New Wave director, co-founder of the movement. Directed The 400 Blows. Also famous as a film critic (Cahiers du Cinema) and for his book-length interview with Alfred Hitchcock. Wrote the essay on 'auteur' theory and a ruthless essay on 'a certain tendency' of French cinema that killed the careers of the directors known as 'Aurenchebost.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Associated with The 400 Blows; one of the main directors of the French New Wave
examples: Directors associated with this movement include Jean-Luc Godard and Francois Truffaut.
which also names a 1960s 'French' movement that produced films like Breathless and The 400 Blows

works.name: Left Bank Directors (Marker, Resnais, Varda)
works.description: The Left Bank faction included Chris Marker (La Jetee, Sans Soleil), Alain Resnais, and Agnes Varda (along with her husband Jacques Demy). Their films were more experimental than Right Bank films. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Left Bank directors include Chris Marker, Alain Resnais, Agnes Varda (and Jacques Demy); more experimental faction of the movement
examples: Marker was often grouped with the Left Bank directors of this French film movement along with Alain Resnais and husband-and-wife Jacques Demy and Agnes Varda.
proponents included Agnes Varda and Jean-Luc Godard

comprehensive_summary:
The French New Wave (Nouvelle Vague) was an influential French film movement of the late 1950s and 1960s that emphasized innovative techniques — handheld cameras, natural lighting, jump cuts, and non-professional actors — and auteur-driven filmmaking. Jean Rouch's Moi, un Noir was explicitly cited as inspiring the movement through its use of jump cuts and non-professional actors. Directors associated with the movement include Jean-Luc Godard, Francois Truffaut, Agnes Varda, Chris Marker, Alain Resnais, and Jacques Demy.

The movement is divided into two factions: the Right Bank, which included more popular and commercially successful directors like Godard and Truffaut; and the Left Bank, which included the more experimental Chris Marker, Alain Resnais, and Agnes Varda (married to Jacques Demy). Jean Vigo was a key predecessor; the Prix Jean Vigo, a prize named for him, was won by Ousmane Sembene for a Wolof-language film.

The seminal film of the movement is Breathless (A bout de souffle, 1960) by Jean-Luc Godard, in which a Humphrey Bogart-obsessed protagonist named Michel kills a police officer after being caught with a stolen car, in a famous jarring five-shot sequence. The 400 Blows (Les Quatre Cents Coups, 1959) by Truffaut is the other canonical film. Godard also directed Pierrot le fou and Vivre sa vie (both starring then-wife Anna Karina) and Alphaville (a neo-noir). Neo-noir films like Le Samourai and Alphaville are called 'neo-noir' within the New Wave tradition.

The French New Wave directly influenced multiple national cinema movements, including the Taiwanese New Wave (Edward Yang, Ang Lee) and had wide international influence.

Director: made Breathless; also directed Pierrot le fou and Vivre sa vie — both starring then-wife Anna Karina; neo-noir Alphaville
